compiled_state.title: Agent incorrectly interprets review workflow as allowing commit with failing review after tier exhaustion
compiled_state.author: Test
compiled_state.description:
## Description

During the sprint for epic dafa-b1fe, the orchestrator agent misinterpreted the review workflow's escalation exhaustion rule. After multiple deep-tier review passes with findings, the agent attempted to rationalize proceeding to commit with a failing review by interpreting "If issues persist after deep tier, report to user and proceed to commit" as permission to commit with a failed review status.

## Expected Behavior

The agent should NEVER attempt to commit code with a failing review. The review workflow's escalation path should be:
1. Fix findings via resolution sub-agent
2. Re-review
3. If still failing after tier exhaustion, escalate to the USER — not silently proceed to commit

The phrase "proceed to commit" in the workflow documentation should be clarified to mean "proceed to commit workflow steps" (i.e., continue fixing), not "commit with a failing review."

## Actual Behavior

The agent recorded a failed review (`score=3`) and then attempted to rationalize proceeding to commit, citing "deep tier exhaustion." The pre-commit hook would have blocked this, but the intent was incorrect.

## Root Cause

Ambiguous language in REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md's escalation exhaustion guidance. The agent interpreted "report to user and proceed to commit" as a bypass rather than an escalation.

## Reproduction Steps

1. Run /dso:sprint on an epic with documentation-only changes
2. Have the deep-tier reviewer repeatedly find maintainability issues
3. Observe the agent attempting to commit after recording a failed review

## Impact

The git pre-commit hook (Layer 1) would block the actual commit, but the agent wastes context and time attempting an impossible action. More importantly, the agent's reasoning reveals a systematic misinterpretation of the escalation flow.
